coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay for ciprofloxacin in human blood serum and urine samples based on sulfur-doped carbon dots/rhodamine B nanohybrid encapsulated HKUST-1 metal-organic framework
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sadeghi-chahnasir, Fatemeh
Amiripour, Fatemeh
Ghasemi, Shahram
coredata.dc:description: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) from biomass waste is of great interest due to its low cost synthesis, environmental compatibility and functionalization without adding dangerous chemicals. Herein, S-doped carbon dot (SCD) was synthesized using agricultural waste as carbon precursors and modified in-situ with rhodamine B dye (SCD@RHB) to construct efficient flouresent probe. SCD@RHB was loaded into HKUST-1 metal-organic framework (SCD@RHB/HKUST-1) and the probe was employed as ratiometric flouresent (RF) sensor for the determination of ciprofloxacin (CIP) antibiotic in trace level. The sensor emits two-resolved peaks at 415 and 575 nm under excitation wavelength of 310 nm which are due to the characteristic fluorescence emissions of SCD and RHB, respectively. Under optimal conditions, the emissions of SCD and RHB change oppositely in the presence of CIP which can be used to evaluate the sensing performance of probe. The ratiometric SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 nanohybrid has a wide linear range (0.1–300 μM) with a low detection limit of 21.3 nM along with good sensitivity and selectivity for CIP. The probe was also used for the determination of CIP in human blood and urine samples. The results demonstrated rapid and accurate analysis with reasonable recoveries of 94 to 102 % for blood sample and 94–106 % for urine sample. This strategy provides a conveninet way to design RF sensors based on HKUST-1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric sensor
HKUST-1
Ciprofloxacin
Rhodamine B
S-doped carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing procedure of CIP by SCD@RHB/HKUST-1
Determination of CIP in blood and urine
Optimization of CIP sensing via ratiometric measurement
Reproducibility and stability of the SCD@RHB/HKUST-1
Determination of CIP in real samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to gratefully appreciate the scientific and technical supports of University of Mazandaran, Research Council, for this research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern, (B) FT-IR spectrum, (C) FESEM and (D) TEM images (two different magnifications) of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1.
(A) EDS spectrum, (B) selected area and (C) elemental mapping of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and MP plots of (A and C) HKUST-1 and (B and D) SCD@RHB/HKUST-1, respectively.

(A) UV–Vis spectra and (B) Emission spectra (λex = 310 nm) of different samples (inset: photographic images of solution (a) before and (b) after irradiation with λex = 365 nm). (C) Fluorescence spectra of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 in (a) absence and (b) presence of CIP (50 μM) in PBS (pH = 8).

(A) UV-Vis absorption and emission spectra of different samples. (B) Effect of different excitation wavelengths on fluorescence intensities of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1. Effect of (C) pH, (D) time and (E) dose of probe on sensing performance of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1.

(A) Fluorescence spectra, (B) calibration curve, and (C) photographic color changes of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 with successive addition of CIP in PBS. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Comparision of fluorescence intensities of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 with time, (B) (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 ratios for probes of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 prepared at same way in presence of 150 μM of CIP during one day. (C) Selectivity and (D) anti-interference ability of probe (CIP = 80 μM).

(A, C) Fluorescence spectra and (B, D) calibration curves of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 recorded with addition of CIP into human blood serum and urine, respectively. Error bars in (B) and (D) represent standard deviations from mean of five replicates.
Different steps for preparation of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1.
Summary of parameters obtained from N2 adsorption-desorption analysis.
Comparison of analytical performance of some fluorescence sensors for CIP determination.
Analytical parameters for determination of CIP using SCD@RHB/HKUST-1.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay for ciprofloxacin in human blood serum and urine samples based on sulfur-doped carbon dots/rhodamine B nanohybrid encapsulated HKUST-1 metal-organic framework

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) from biomass waste is of great interest due to its low cost synthesis, environmental compatibility and functionalization without adding dangerous chemicals. Herein, S-doped carbon dot (SCD) was synthesized using agricultural waste as carbon precursors and modified in-situ with rhodamine B dye (SCD@RHB) to construct efficient flouresent probe. SCD@RHB was loaded into HKUST-1 metal-organic framework (SCD@RHB/HKUST-1) and the probe was employed as ratiometric flouresent (RF) sensor for the determination of ciprofloxacin (CIP) antibiotic in trace level. The sensor emits two-resolved peaks at 415 and 575 nm under excitation wavelength of 310 nm which are due to the characteristic fluorescence emissions of SCD and RHB, respectively. Under optimal conditions, the emissions of SCD and RHB change oppositely in the presence of CIP which can be used to evaluate the sensing performance of probe. The ratiometric SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 nanohybrid has a wide linear range (0.1–300 μM) with a low detection limit of 21.3 nM along with good sensitivity and selectivity for CIP. The probe was also used for the determination of CIP in human blood and urine samples. The results demonstrated rapid and accurate analysis with reasonable recoveries of 94 to 102 % for blood sample and 94–106 % for urine sample. This strategy provides a conveninet way to design RF sensors based on HKUST-1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) is one of the most important fluoroquinolone antibiotics of the second generation, which is used as a vital antibiotic in the treatment of animal and human diseases [1,2]. This antibiotic is used to treat various diseases such as respiratory, digestive, urinary and skin infections as well as chronic bacterial prostatitis [3–5]. Excessive use of CIP causes severe side effects such as liver poisoning and central nervous system disorders [6,7]. On the other hand, studies showed that 70 % of antibiotics leak into the environment and has serious effects on ecology. Recently, due to the overuse of CIP, more attention has been drawn to the design and construction of a simple and sensitive pathway for its measurement [8]. To measure and detect CIP, some methods such as capillary electrophoresis [9], electrochemical method [10], and high-performance liquid chromatography [11] with high sensitivity and accuracy have been used [6]. However, the main problems of these techniques such as complexity, expensiveness and consuming sample handling procedures hinder the application of on-site real-time rapid detection in routine frequent detection. Fluorimetric detection have been noticed as cheapness and reliable method, with excellent sensitivity and simplicity [12].

Among the fluorescence probes, oxygenated carbon dots (CDs) are emerged as a new class of light-emitting materials due to their attractive features such as easy synthesis process, simple surface modification, unique optical properties, excellent environmental compatibility, and less toxicity [13,14]. CDs have achieved great success in various fields such as biomedicine [15], solar cells [16], and biocompatible imaging [17]. However, due to easy accumulation, low selectivity and instability, CDs always need further modification to control and suppress these disadvantages [18].

In the last two decades, metal-organic frameworks (MOF) have become a new class of crystalline materials due to their unique properties such as high surface area, rich porosity and adjustable skeletal structure, which are the result of the bonding of allied ligands with metal ions [19]. Due to their capabilities, these materials have shown many potential applications in various fields such as separation and gas absorption, chemical sensing, molecular separation, drug delivery, and fluorescence sensors [20].

Cu-BTC MOF namely as HKUST-1 by chemical formula of Cu₃(C₉H₆O₆)₂ has a three-dimensional structure formed by weak coordination between the organic linker benzene 1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (BTC) and Cu2+ [21,22]. Due to high surface area of HKUST-1, it can be as a favorable support for encapsulating CDs guests to hinder their accumulation which can also improve the selectivity and stability of probe. Moreover, HKUST-1 can accumulate more analyte on the surface and amplify the signal of sensor [23].

Most CDs used as fluorescence probes are based on single-emissive and have not suitable sensitivity for sensing purpose owing to some factors such as environmental obtrusion, inhomogeneous distribution of the analyte, and operational errors [24,25]. It is known that double or multi-emissive response signals can minimize the aforementioned adverse effects and a ratiometric fluorescent (RF) probe can be constructed based on the relationship between the ratio of multi-wavelength fluorescence intensities [26]. In this regard, encapsulating the secondary fluorophores such as dye molecules into the HKUST-1 framework can prepare sensors with greater accuracy and precision than those based on single-emissive probes [27]. In this research, sulfur-doped CDs (SCDs) were synthesized from an agricultural waste biomass through an efficient alternative route and then, modified with thiourea to prepare important fluorescent materials [28]. SCDs and rhodamine B as secondary fluorophores were embedded in the MOF structure, and as a result, the sensor based on SCD@RHB and HKUST-1 was fabricated for determination of CIP through the ratiometric signal. Because of high specific surface area of HKUST-1, SCDs and RB are well dispersed in MOFs which hinderes any further leakage of them into solution and improve the photostability of sensor. SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 has two-resolved peak at the region of 415 and 576 nm upon the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. While the first peak increases in the presence of CIP, the second one decreases, so, RF sensor is made by considering the emission ratio at two wavelengths. SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 has low detection limit, wide linear range, high selectivity, and sensitivity for CIP assay. This study can be considered as a new strategy to developed RF sensor based on the combination of HKUST-1 with large specific surface area and the unique optical properties of SCD@RHB.
Information on chemicals and instrumentations is provided in supporting materials.

Firstly, agricultural waste (50 mg) as source of CD, rhodamine B (50 mg), and thiourea (50 mg) were sonicated in 80 mL of deionized water for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling of autoclave, the impurities were removed via centrifuging. In next step, a 0.22 μm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane syringe head filter (REF. PTFE033S022I) was used for further purification. The resulting clear solution was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use [29].

SCD@RHB (13 mL) was mixed with ethanol (4.25 mL), deionized water (4.25 mL), and DMF (4.25 mL) and stirred for 15 min. After that, a mixture of 0.25 g of BTC and 0.5 g of CuNO3.3H2O was added to the solution and stirred for 15 min [30]. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated for 24 h at 85 °C. Then, the dark blue powder was obtained by centrifugation and washed three times with DMF, deionized water and dichloromethane. The different steps of synthesis process are given in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing procedure of CIP by SCD@RHB/HKUST-1
ce:section.ce:para: To study the fluorescence property of the SCD@RHB/HKUST-1, first, 5 mg of sample was added to 5 mL of phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 8) and irradiated with ultrasonic waves for 5 min. Then 3 mL of solution was poured into the cell of spectrofluorometer and the excitation wavelength was set to 310 nm. Also, bandwidth for excitation and emission was set to 10:10 respectively, and the fluorescence response of probe was recorded with addition of CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CIP in blood and urine
ce:section.ce:para: To study the applicability of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 for real sample analysis, human blood serum was collected from clinical laboratory (Babolsar, Mazandaran province, Iran). At first, serum (15 mL) was centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 15 min and the upper solution was again filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The specific volume of filtrate (1 mL) was diluted 50 times with PBS (pH = 8). Afterward, SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 (5 mg) was dispersed into 5 mL of the solution and sonicated for 15 min [31]. Then, different volumes of CIP standard solution were spiked to the pretreated human blood serum and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

For pretreatment on urine, 3 mL of methanol was added to the sample. In this step, the urine protein was precipitated and removed via centrifuging at a speed of 7500 rpm for 25 min. The solution was further purified with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then, the specific volume (0.5 mL) was added to the 50 mL of PBS (pH = 8). The following steps were continued according to the pretreatment of blood serum sample [32].

The XRD pattern of sample is shown in Fig. 1 A. The crystal planes appeared at the pattern confirms the pyramidal structure of the SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 network [33]. The diffraction pattern of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 matches well with that for HKUST-1, indicating that the incorporation of SCD@RHB can not destroy the crystal structure of host. In addition, with incorporation of small percentage of SCD@RHB to MOF, no obvious peak related to SCD@RHB is observed in the nanohybrid pattern.

In FT-IR spectrum of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 (Fig. 1B), there are stretching vibrational bands of CuO at 476 and 764 cm −1. Also, the sharp peak at 2920 cm −1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–H. The absorption peaks at 1635 and 1371 cm −1 indicate the stretching vibrations of CO and C–O and the presence of O–H bending functional groups in the carboxylic group, respectively [34]. Several bands can be seen at wavenumbers of around 665, 729 and 1109 cm−1, which are related to S–S, and C–S stretchings for two next bands, respectively. In addition, the couple bands at 1444 and 1565 cm−1 are related to N–H and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively [35].

FESEM images of HKUST-1 (Fig. S1 A) show that the morphology of sample is composed of pyramidal cystals which have distinct edges and smooth surfaces with grooves. Moreover, no significant change can be observed in the morphology of HKUST-1 after modification by SCD@RHB (Fig. 1C). Although some nanoparticles appeare on the surface of HKUST-1, the stability of HKUST-1 is preseeved. However, the amount of groove on the surface of HKUST-1 has decreased after modification, which can be attributed to the presence of SCD@RHB which fills them.

TEM images of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 are shown in Fig. 1D. It seems that in addition to the surface, SCD@RHB nanoparticles may enter into HKUST-1 structure. The histogram of particle size also shows that SCDs have a narrow distribution with an average size of about 6–9 nm (Fig. S1 B).

Elemental analysis was used to prove the presence of different elements and investigate the chemical composition in SCD@RHB/HKUST-1. As can be seen, Kα lines related to Cu, O, N, C and S elements are presented in the EDS spectrum (Fig. 2 A). Besides, elemental mapping (Fig. 2C) confirms the homogenous distribution of elements in the selected region of the SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 shown by FESEM (Fig. 2B). The weight percentage of each element in the sample is given in the Table S1. As shown in the table, C and O elements have the highest weight percent, along with the presence of large percentage of Cu which indicate the successful embedding of SCDs in the HKUST-1 structure. On the other hand, the presence of N and S in the elemental analysis also indicates the presence of SCD and RHB in the structure of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1.

The textural properties of HKUST-1 and SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 samples were analyzed by N2 adsorption-desorption at 77 K (Fig. 3 A and B). As can be seen, the isotherms correspond to type I according to IUPAC classification, which is characteristic of microporous materials. Also, the residual loop is not observed in the isotherms and shows the formation of homogeneous pores [36]. Besides, the isotherm of HKUST-1 shows higher volume of absorbed N2 gas than that for SCD@RHB/HKUST-1, which is attributed to the blocking of pores in HKUST-1 as a result of nanohybrid formation. Also, Fig. 3C and D shows the MP plot of HKUST-1 and SCD@RHB/HKUST-1. Table 1 provides a summary of the results obtained from N2 adsorption/desorption analysis. As can be seen, the specific surface area of HKUST-1 and SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 were calculated as 1160.13 and 928.43 m2 g−1, respectively. Both samples have high surface area, but after introducing SCD@RHB in HKUST-1 structure, its surface area decreases, which is more evidence for formation of nanohybrid.

The optical properties of SCD@RHB, HKUST-1 and SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 were evaluated through UV–Vis spectroscopy. As can be seen in the Fig. 4 A, the SCD@RHB spectrum shows two absorption peaks at the wavelengths of 260 and 360 nm due to the π→π∗ transition of aromatic moieties and the n→π∗ transition of CO bonds, respectively [37–39]. Besides, two characteristic absorption peaks with wavelenghths in the range of 490-560 nm are due to rhodamine B [40]. On the other hand, HKUST-1 has a strong absorption peak at 230 nm that assigns to the π -π∗ transition of the Cu-oxo cluster (Fig. 4A). It seems that the absorption spectrum of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 has the characteristic absorption peaks of SCD@RHB and HKUST-1 which indicates the successful loading of SCD@RHB into the HKUST-1 structure.

The fluorescence spectra of SCD@RHB, HKUST-1, and SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 upon the excitation wavelength of 310 nm is presented in Fig. 4B. As can be seen, SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 emits fluorescence with lower intensity than SCD@RHB. Usually, metal ions such as Fe2+ and Cu2+ cause to the static quenching of the CD emission. So, it can be concluded that the weak emission of the SCD@RHB/HKUST may be raised through the interaction of Cu (II) in HKUST-1 with S or OH containing functional groups on the SCD@RHB which results in electron transfer from the photoexcited SCDs to copper ions [41]. The inset indicates the photographic images of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 under visible light and UV lamp (λex = 365 nm).

The performance of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 for fluorometric determination of CIP is compared with that for SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 in Fig. 4C. With addition of CIP (50 μM), the intensity of two peaks appeared at spectrum of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 changes so the intensity of peak related to SCD increases while the emission of RHB decreases. The fluorescence of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 originats from the SCD@RHB nanoparticles which are embedded in HKUST-1 architecture. Both HKUST-1 and SCD@RHB play an important role in improving the sensitivity and selectivity of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 for determination of CIP. HKUST-1 can increase the accumulation of CIP on the probe surface and improve its sensitivity to analyte [41]. On the other hand, as an ideal host, it prevents the adhesion of SCD@RHB nanoparticles to each others through encapsulation in the HKUST-1 structure and improves their optical stability. With amazing optical properties of SCD@RHB and excellent adsorption capability of HKUST-1, it is possible to prepare a sensor with good performance for determination of CIP.

To better understand the sensing mechanism, the absorption spectrum of CIP was recorded and the results are shown in Fig. 5 A. In the spectrum, CIP shows double strong absorption peaks at 275 and 330 nm, which have no overlap with the emission of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1. Therefore, the sensing mechanism can not be related to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and it is assumed that the sensor can detect CIP through the Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) mechanism. This phenomenon leads to tunable fluorescence emission property for SCD@RHB/HKUST-1. In order to better investigate the fluorescence mechanism, the UV–Vis spectrum was taken from the probe solution before and after adding CIP. It is observed that two recorded spectra are not different in the peak positions; hence, probe and CIP are not bonding together chemically [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CIP sensing via ratiometric measurement
ce:section.ce:para: To choose the most appropriate excitation wavelength on the emission of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at different wavelengths (250–370 nm) (Fig. 5B). As previously mentioned, the proposed RF sensor has two emission peaks at wavelength of 415 and 576 nm, which are respectively related to the two guest fluorophores of SCD and RHB embedded in the HKUST-1. In the presence of CIP, an increase and quenching of fluorescence intensities of SCD and RHB occurred, respectively. According to Fig. 5B, the excitation wavelength of 310 nm was selected as the optimal wavelength because the probe has the lowest and highest intensites at 415 nm and 576 nm, respectively, which provide the best sensing performance for determination of CIP. SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 has different fluorescence intensity in various excitation wavelength, which may be due to the heterogeneous size of SCD@RHB, or multiple functional groups on the surface [43]. These groups may act as energy traps or transfer energy to non-fluorescent materials causing the change in fluorescence emission [44,45].

The optimization process of parameters such as pH, time and amount of nanohybrid that affect onto the sensing performance of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 are followed up to achieve the maximum sensitivity and accuracy. To find the optimal pH, the ratio of emission intensities of fluorescence peak at 415 nm (F415) and 576 nm (F576) at different pH values in the absence (F0) and presence of constant concentration of CIP (50 μM) was measured from the spectrum and then the plot of (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 versus pH is drawn (Fig. 5C). The results show that the values of (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 decrease at pH lower or higher than 8 which can be attributed to the accumulation of SCD@RHB or lower affinity of CIP to the binding sites of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 at high concenteration of H+ or OH – [46].

The sensor response time is very important issue for determination of the analyte. As shown in Fig. 5D, (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 ratio increases immediately after addition of CIP up to 5 min, but little change can be observed during longer interaction time due to the saturation of binding sites. To save time and due to the high amount of (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 ratio obtained after 5 min of incubation at pH = 8, the following experiments were done at this time.

To study the effect of probe concenteration (3, 5, 7 and 9 mg dispersed in 5 mL PBS) on sensing performance of nanohybrid, the values of (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 ratio were calculated from the fluorescence spectra provided in the absence and presence of CIP (50 μM). It can be seen that 5 mg of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 provides the highest ratio and therefore was selected for further investigations (Fig. 5E).

As can be seen in the Fig. 6 A, with increasing the CIP concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 at wavelenghth of 415 nm grows up clearly, while the emission of RHB at 576 nm decreases. The calibration curve is obtained with plotting the (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 values against different concentrations of CIP (Fig. 6B). A good linear relationship is observed in the concentration range of 0.1–300 μM with a correlation coefficient of R2=0.9992. The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3 times of standard deviation of the blank signal (S/N = 3) is equal to 21.3 nM for determination of CIP [47]. Also, the sensitivity of the proposed fluorescence sensor is obtained to be 0.0811 μM−1. The photographic images of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 solution after addition of different concentrations of CIP is presented in Fig. 6C. Table 2 compares the analytical performance of the proposed sensor for determination of CIP with other research works. It can be seen that SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 has wide linear range, low LOD, and high sensitivity which makes it promising candidate for determination of CIP in aqeous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the SCD@RHB/HKUST-1
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the optical stability of the sensor, 5 mg of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 was weighed and added to 5 mL of PBS buffer with pH = 8, and then its fluorescence spectra were recorded at different interval times (Fig. 7 A). In the period of 0–2 min, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor decreases, but in time range of 3–4 min, its response becomes stable. This demonstrates that the fluorescence emission of the SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 can stabilize in a short time. To evaluate the reproducibility, four probes were prepared at same way and their fluorescence spectra were recorded in the presence of CIP (150 μM). The results showed that there is no significant difference in their responses to CIP (Fig. 7B).

In order to investigate the selectivity of sensor, the response of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 was examine toward 10 μM of CIP, leucine (Leu), glucose (Gluc), histidine (His), l-cysteine (L-Cy), glycine (Gly), Cd2+, Fe3+, Pb2+ and sodium dichromate (Fig. 7C). The F415/F576 ratio greatly increases with addition of 80 μM of CIP. However, almost no change in response (F415/F576) is observed after the addition of interfering species. In addition, the (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 value for CIP was obtained in the presence of the same concentrations (80 μM) of Leu, Gluc, L-Cy, glutamine (Glut), sodium dichromate and Gly (Fig. 7D). No significant change in the flouresence ratio is observed in the presence of other species which indicates its superior anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CIP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human blood serum and urine samples were used to evaluate the applicability of the developed sensor. The samples are pretreated according to section 2.4, and the fluorescence spectra were recoded in the presence of spiked CIP and the changes in (F415/F576)/(F415/F576)0 with concentration was plotted. The LOD and sensitivity of the sensor in blood serum were 2.37 and 0.3478 μM−1, respectively (Fig. 8 A and B). Also for the urine sample, LOD of 0.065 μM−1 and sensitivity of 0.1078 μM−1 were obtained (Fig. 8C and D). As shown in Table 3 , the recoveries for blood serum and urine were calculated in the range of 94–102 % and 94–106 %, respectively, which indicates the proper performance of SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 probe in complex matrix.

A ratiometric SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 fluorescence sensor was fabricated through the modification of HKUST-1 with dual probe of SCD@RHB to provide selective and sensitive assay for CIP as an effective fluoroquinolone antibiotic. The results confirmed the successful encapsulation of SCD@RHB nanoparticles into HKUST-1 metal-organic framework. SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 with large specific surface area and high total pore volume induces excellent ability to adsorb CIP. The results showed that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor has high sensitivity with low LOD which can selectively detect CIP in the presence of other biomolecules and some cations. The photo-stability experiments demonstrate that the probe has stable response for determination of CIP in human blood serum and urine samples with satisfactory accuracy and recovery. Based on the results, the proposed SCD@RHB/HKUST-1 sensor can be used as a cost-effective, efficient and selective sensor for determination of CIP in real samples.

Fatemeh Sadeghi-chahnasir: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Fatemeh Amiripour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shahram Ghasemi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to gratefully appreciate the scientific and technical supports of University of Mazandaran, Research Council, for this research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.127477.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stable europium-based metal–organic frameworks for naked-eye ultrasensitive detecting fluoroquinolones antibiotics
Inorg. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xiao, M. Liu, F. Tian, Z. Liu, Stable Europium-based Metal-Organic Frameworks for Naked-eye Ultrasensitive Detecting Fluoroquinolones Antibiotics, Inorg. Chem. (2021) 60, 5282-5289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective ciprofloxacin antibiotic detection by fluorescent siderophore pyoverdin
Biosens. Bioelectron.
